era_context: The year 573 CE falls within the reign of Byzantine Emperor Justin II (565–578 CE), the Sasanid Empire under Khosrow I Anushirvan (531–579 CE), and the declining Northern Zhou Dynasty in China. The Byzantine-Sasanid Wars were ongoing, with both empires experiencing military and financial strain. In Europe, the Merovingian Frankish kingdoms were engaged in dynastic struggles. This period represents a transitional moment in world history, with major empires facing internal challenges and external pressures.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond the Frankish realm

events.title: Byzantine-Sasanid War: Continued Mesopotamian Conflict
events.description: The Byzantine-Sasanid Wars continued in 573 CE with ongoing military operations in Mesopotamia. Both empires were engaged in a costly struggle for control of the region, with fortified cities like Dara, Nisibis, and Ctesiphon serving as focal points of conflict. The wars had been ongoing since 540 CE and would continue until 591 CE. By 573, both empires were experiencing financial exhaustion, which would eventually drive them toward negotiated peace.
events.certainty_note: The continuation of Byzantine-Sasanid warfare in the 570s is well-attested, but specific events in 573 are not always precisely dated. The broader pattern is clear; the exact chronology is approximate.
cross_references: Part of the broader Byzantine-Sasanid Wars (540–591 CE)

events.title: Frankish Kingdom Conflicts: Merovingian Civil Wars
events.description: The Merovingian Frankish kingdoms were engaged in ongoing dynastic conflicts in 573 CE. King Sigebert I of Austrasia and King Chilperic I of Neustria were rivals for dominance. These conflicts, known as the Merovingian civil wars, involved military campaigns, diplomatic maneuvering, and the involvement of powerful aristocratic families. The wars shaped the political landscape of early medieval Europe and would continue for decades.

sources.name: Gregory of Tours, History of the Franks

events.certainty_note: Gregory of Tours provides detailed accounts of Frankish conflicts in the 570s. His work is the primary source for this period.

events.title: Northern Zhou Dynasty Internal Strife
events.description: The Northern Zhou Dynasty was experiencing internal strife and factional conflicts in 573 CE. Emperor Wu of Northern Zhou was still alive but aging, and power struggles were emerging among court factions. The dynasty was visibly weakening, with the rise of Yang Jian as a power broker. By 573, the Northern Zhou's days were clearly numbered, though the dynasty would not formally collapse until 581 CE.
key_figures: Emperor Wu of Northern Zhou (r. 561–578 CE)

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: The decline of Northern Zhou in the 570s is well-documented in Chinese historical records.
cross_references: Leads to Sui Dynasty unification in 581 CE

disconfirming_evidence: No major disconfirming evidence undermines the events listed for 573 CE. However, the precise dating of specific military operations in the Byzantine-Sasanid Wars is uncertain, and sources do not always clearly distinguish events in 573 from those in adjacent years. The significance of Frankish conflicts in 573 specifically is difficult to isolate from the broader pattern of Merovingian civil wars.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 573 CE is moderate, with strong documentation for the Byzantine Empire, Frankish kingdoms, and China, but sparse sources for other regions. Byzantine sources (Menander, Theophylact) are later compilations and reflect court perspectives. Gregory of Tours is the primary source for Frankish history, written from a Frankish Christian perspective. Chinese sources are systematic but reflect dynastic historiography. The record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and the Americas are essentially absent from the written record.

graph_edges.note: Part of the broader Byzantine-Sasanid Wars (540–591 CE)
Merovingian civil wars continued; Sigebert I died in 574/575 CE
